did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper is titled 'A Comparative Study of various Skin Cancer using Deep Learning Techniques' and explicitly states it performs a comparative study of deep learning algorithms (CNNs, MLPs, ANN, DT) for skin cancer detection and classification using dermoscopic images. However, the paper was retracted by IEEE due to incoherent technical language impacting result reliability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is titled 'A Comparative Study of various Skin Cancer using Deep Learning Techniques' and was published in the '2022 International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Sustainable Engineering Solutions (CISES)'. It presents original experimental results comparing the performance of various deep learning algorithms (CNN, MLP, ANN, DT, etc.) on skin cancer datasets, as evidenced by the 'Result' section listing accuracy metrics for these models. Although the paper was later retracted by IEEE due to incoherent technical language, it was originally intended and structured as a research paper presenting new experimental findings rather than a review paper.

title: A Comparative Study of various Skin Cancer using Deep Learning Techniques
journal: 2022 International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Sustainable Engineering Solutions (CISES)
brief_summary: This retracted 2022 conference paper presents a comparative study of deep learning techniques, including CNNs, MLPs, ANNs, and GANs, for skin cancer detection using dermoscopic images. The authors reviewed various public datasets such as HAM10000, PH2, ISIC, DermQuest, and DermIS to evaluate model performance. The study aimed to identify the most effective algorithm for distinguishing between benign and malignant skin lesions, specifically melanoma and non-melanoma types. Results were compiled in tables showing accuracy metrics for different classifiers across these datasets, with the authors concluding that CNNs performed best. However, IEEE retracted the article due to incoherent technical language that impacted the reliability of the reported results.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as a lethal malignant growth and one of the deadliest types of skin cancer. Used as a primary class in the ISIC dataset (harmful melanoma) and PH2 dataset (40 pictures of melanoma skin disease). The study focuses on distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions.
Categorized as one of the three main types of skin cancer (along with SCC and melanoma). Described as an unusual cell created in the area of the body impacted by UV radiation.
Categorized as the second sort of skin malignant growth. Mentioned as one of the three main types of skin cancer.

Used as a benign comparator class. The PH2 dataset contains 80 pictures of nevus (listed twice in text, likely implying two types or a typo, but explicitly stated as nevus). The ISIC subset contains harmless nevus class. The text notes difficulty in distinguishing melanoma and nevus due to visual similarity.

Used as a broad category for classification tasks, specifically distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma skin malignant growth. The paper mentions 3.3 million cases of non-melanoma skin cancer annually.
Listed as a different kind of skin disease/malignant growth in the introduction, but not explicitly stated as a target class for the deep learning experiments.
Listed as a different kind of skin disease/malignant growth in the introduction, but not explicitly stated as a target class for the deep learning experiments.
Listed as a different kind of skin disease in the introduction, but not explicitly stated as a target class for the deep learning experiments.
Referenced as a general category for non-malignant lesions, particularly in the context of the ISIC dataset (harmless nevus) and general classification tasks distinguishing benign from malignant.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (e.g., melanoma vs. nevus, BCC vs. non-BCC, benign vs. malignant, melanoma vs. non-melanoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states it performs 'skin cancer detection' and 'classification' using deep learning techniques (CNNs, MLPs, ANNs, DTs) to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions, specifically melanoma and non-melanoma types. The results section reports 'Accuracy (%)' metrics for these classification tasks across various datasets (ISIC, PH2, DermQuest, etc.).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public skin lesions dataset containing dermoscopy images. The paper notes it contains 10,015 dermatoscopy images edited and saved at 72dpi with a resolution of 800x600 pixels.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (CNN, ANN) in comparative studies.
use.other: Pooled with other datasets to generate a single skin cancer dataset for evaluation in related work.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 200 dermoscopy pictures with a resolution of 768 x 560 pixels. It includes clinical explanations, histological analysis, and dermatoscopy standards.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (CNN, GAN).

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A collection of various datasets on skin sores. The paper specifically references ISIC2016 and ISIC2017/2018 subsets.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (CNN, GAN, SVM).
use.other: Referenced as a growing record of images.

datasets_used.name: DermQuest
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing dermatoscopy pictures from all dermatoscopy datasets with 134 injury labels.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (CNN).

datasets_used.name: DermIS
datasets_used.description: A dermatology information system dataset containing dermatoscopy pictures, case reports, and key findings.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (SVM).

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing pictures of skin sicknesses, biologically partitioned into a 2-layered arrangement.

use.other: Listed as a type of dataset used for comparison of deep learning techniques.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper presents a comparative study of deep learning algorithms using accuracy metrics without statistical significance tests.

HAM10000.notes: The paper presents a comparative study of deep learning techniques. The results listed in Figure 2 and the accompanying text are attributed to the HAM10000 dataset in the context of the study's methodology section which discusses this dataset. The paper was retracted due to incoherent technical language impacting result reliability.

architecture_used.main_model: The paper reports a comparative study of multiple architectures including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs), Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Decision Trees (DTs), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Support Vector Machines (SVMs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and Kohonen Self-Organizing Maps (SOM). Specific model variants mentioned include Deep CNN, SVM-trained with CNN, Self-attention-based PGAN, and ANN with backpropagation.
architecture_used.pretraining: The paper mentions the use of 'pre-trained deep learning network with transfer learning' in the context of related work [14], but does not specify pretraining details for the models used in its own comparative results. It references 'Alex net and VGG16 deep features' in related work [16].
architecture_used.input_size: Null. The paper describes dataset resolutions (e.g., HAM10000: 800x600 pixels; PH2: 768x560 pixels) but does not specify the input tensor dimensions used for the models in the comparative experiments.

architecture_details.CNN: Described as a blend of a pooling layer and a convolutional layer, followed by a fully connected layer. Used for image classification.
architecture_details.ANN: Described as having an input layer, hidden layers, and an output layer. Specific instance: 'ANN with backpropagation algorithm'.
architecture_details.GAN: Described as having a generator module and a discriminator module. Specific instance: 'Self-attention-based PGAN'.
architecture_details.Kohonen SOM: Described as having two layers: an input layer and a conflict layer, fully associated.
architecture_details.SVM: Used as a classifier, sometimes in combination with CNN features ('SVM trained with CNN').
architecture_details.Decision Tree: Listed as one of the algorithms compared.

classification_or_output_head.details: The models output class labels for skin cancer diagnosis (e.g., Melanoma, Nevus, BCC, Benign Keratosis). Specific outputs mentioned include 'Melanoma/nevu', 'BCC/non-BCC', 'AK/BCC/benign keratosis', and 'Melanoma/Non-melanoma'.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Null. The paper does not report parameter counts for the models.

preprocessing_techniques: The paper mentions that pre-processing techniques like median filtering and contrast stretching are used for noise reduction and picture enhancement in related work [17].
The paper notes that images were physically edited and saved at 72dpi with specific resolutions (e.g., 800x600 pixels for HAM10000, 768x560 pixels for PH2) in the dataset descriptions.
The paper references threshold-based segmentation for isolating affected regions in related work [17].
The paper references Biorthogonal 2-D wavelet transform for feature extraction in related work [16].
The paper references Gray-level Co-occurrence Formulation (GLCM) features in related work [15].
The paper references Color histogram and Nonlinear functions as feature sets in related work [15].
The paper references PCA (Principal Component Analysis) for identifying best features in related work [16].
The paper references Alex net and VGG16 deep features extraction in related work [16].
The paper references Self-attention-based PGAN in related work [14] (context unclear if preprocessing or model).
conclusions: The study concludes that deep learning techniques, particularly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), are effective for skin cancer detection and classification, offering better results than traditional machine learning algorithms.

Among the tested algorithms, the Deep CNN model achieved the highest accuracy (98.79%), followed by the ANN (96.9%) and SVM trained with CNN (93.75%), indicating superior performance of deep learning architectures for this task.
The paper acknowledges that skin cancer, particularly melanoma, is a lethal and increasing global health issue, and early detection via automated systems can significantly improve survival rates.

The authors note that distinguishing between melanoma and benign lesions (like nevi) is difficult due to visual similarities in color, shape, and size, necessitating advanced feature extraction and classification methods.
The study highlights the utility of dermoscopic images for providing magnified views of skin lesions that are invisible to the naked eye, thereby aiding in accurate diagnosis.
The paper identifies limitations in existing methods, such as the tedious nature of manual analysis and the need for reliable, automated systems to reduce diagnostic time and labor costs.
The authors conclude that combining human expertise with artificial intelligence classifiers can produce better outcomes in skin cancer categorization than either alone.

The study acknowledges that while deep learning has improved classification accuracy, challenges remain in handling the variety of skin lesion attributes and the need for robust datasets like HAM10000, PH2, and ISIC.
important_remarks: The paper was officially retracted by IEEE due to incoherent, non-standard, or unclear technical language ('tortured phrases') that impacts the reliability of the results.
The authors did not respond to contact regarding the retraction.
The study lacks statistical significance testing, relying solely on accuracy metrics for comparison.
The paper does not report specific hyperparameters, loss functions, optimizers, or training schedules for the deep learning models.
The paper does not specify the input tensor dimensions used for the models, only the dataset resolutions.
The paper does not report parameter counts for the architectures tested.
The PH2 dataset description contains a likely typo, listing '80 pictures of nevus' twice instead of distinguishing between benign and malignant classes clearly in that specific count.

The study pools multiple public datasets (HAM10000, PH2, ISIC, DermQuest, DermIS, DermNet) without clearly defining the specific train/validation/test splits for the comparative experiments presented.

The results section attributes specific accuracy scores (e.g., Deep CNN 98.79%) to the HAM10000 dataset, but the methodology for how these specific models were trained on this specific dataset is not detailed in the provided context.
